title: UI/UX Design Software Pricing Breakdown
description: A pricing analysis of UI/UX design tools — Figma, Sketch, Canva, Framer, Webflow, and InVision — covering tier structures, cost inflection points, and where teams actually overspend.
body:
UI/UX design tool pricing has matured into predictable tiers, but the details that matter most — what each tier includes, where costs scale, and where hidden fees lurk — require careful examination. The published price is rarely the total cost, and teams that select tools based on sticker price alone frequently discover that the real cost includes migration overhead, workflow friction, and feature gaps that require supplementary tools.

This analysis examines the pricing structure of six UI/UX design tools, identifies the cost inflection points where teams typically upgrade, and highlights the pricing traps that catch buyers off guard. All Figma pricing reflects publicly available rates verified in July 2026. Pricing for other tools is based on published rates at the time of writing — check each vendor's current pricing page for exact rates.

## Figma (Verified Pricing)

Figma's pricing is the reference point for the UI/UX design tool market. The Free plan supports unlimited editors with up to three files — sufficient for evaluation but insufficient for active teams. The Professional plan at $16 per editor per month (annual billing; $20 monthly) unlocks unlimited files, branch and merge, Dev Mode, and team libraries. The Organization plan at $55 per editor per month adds design system analytics, centralized administration, private plugins, and advanced branching. The Enterprise plan at $90 per editor per month includes SSO, SCIM, dedicated support, and advanced security.

The cost inflection point in Figma occurs when a team exceeds three files on the Free plan. The transition to Professional represents a meaningful per-editor cost that scales linearly with team size. The second inflection point occurs when the team needs design system analytics or centralized administration, triggering an upgrade to Organization.

Figma's pricing model scales linearly with team size — each additional editor adds the same per-editor monthly cost. This predictability is an advantage for budget planning, but it means that design team growth directly increases software cost.

## Sketch

Sketch's pricing transitioned from one-time purchases to subscriptions. The Free tier provides basic editing with limited collaboration. Paid tiers provide the full editor with updates, and higher tiers add cloud collaboration and shared libraries. Enterprise pricing is custom.

Sketch's pricing advantage is its lower per-user cost compared to Figma for Mac-only teams. The limitation is that Sketch's collaboration features require higher tiers, whereas Figma includes collaboration in the Professional tier.

The pricing trap in Sketch is the update model. Lower tiers provide one year of updates; after that, designers must either continue using the current version without updates or purchase another year. Teams that skip update years accumulate version gaps that complicate collaboration.

Check Sketch's current pricing page for exact rates on Standard and Business tiers.

## Canva

Canva's pricing targets content production rather than product design. The Free tier provides templates, stock photos, and basic design tools. Paid tiers add brand kits, background removal, a larger template library, and team collaboration features. Enterprise pricing is custom.

Canva's pricing trap is the Teams tier minimum. Small teams may need to pay for more seats than they have to access collaboration features. The Pro tier is the practical floor for team use, but it may lack the shared brand kit functionality that teams need for consistency.

For UI/UX teams specifically, Canva's pricing is relevant only for marketing collateral and presentation design. Canva does not support the vector editing, component systems, or prototyping that product design requires, making its UI/UX utility limited.

Check Canva's current pricing page for exact rates on Pro and Teams tiers.

## Framer

Framer's pricing is per-site rather than per-user, creating a fundamentally different cost structure. The Free tier provides basic page building with Framer branding. Paid tiers remove branding and add custom domains, form submissions, and custom code. The Pro tier uses custom pricing.

Framer's per-site model means that costs accumulate with each additional page regardless of team size. This model works for marketing teams but poorly for design teams that need many files for component exploration, design system development, and prototyping.

The pricing trap in Framer is the accumulation of site costs. Each additional page increases the monthly bill, and the lack of a bundled multi-site plan means that marketing-heavy organizations face escalating costs as they produce more pages.

Check Framer's current pricing page for exact rates on Mini, Basic, and Pro tiers.

## Webflow

Webflow's pricing combines design tool capability with hosting infrastructure. The Free tier provides basic site building. Paid tiers remove branding and add custom domains, CMS hosting, form submissions, and API access. Enterprise pricing is custom.

Webflow's pricing reflects its dual identity — teams pay for both the design tool and the hosting platform. Teams that use Webflow for design but deploy to external hosting still pay for the design capability, creating a cost that does not align with actual usage.

The pricing trap in Webflow is the CMS tier limits. Sites with large content collections exceed the CMS tier limits and require higher tiers. Organizations with many content-heavy sites face significant cost accumulation.

Check Webflow's current pricing page for exact rates across Starter, Basic, CMS, and Business tiers.

## InVision

InVision's pricing reflects its focus on design review and collaboration. The Free tier provides basic prototyping and review. Paid tiers add advanced prototyping, unlimited boards, and priority support. Enterprise pricing is custom.

InVision's pricing advantage is its competitive per-user cost for teams that primarily need design review and feedback workflows rather than collaborative design editing. The limitation is that InVision's prototyping capabilities are significantly less sophisticated than Figma's, making it a review tool rather than a design creation tool for most teams.

The pricing trap in InVision is the capability gap. The subscription cost is attractive, but teams that need interactive prototyping with conditional logic will find InVision insufficient and ultimately supplement it with other tools, increasing total cost.

Check InVision's current pricing page for exact rates on the Pro tier.

## Where Teams Overspend

The most common pricing mistake is purchasing features the team does not yet need. Enterprise features like SSO, SCIM, and advanced analytics are irrelevant for teams under 20 people and add significant cost without value. Teams should start with the tier that covers current needs and upgrade when specific capabilities become necessary.

The second most common mistake is underestimating migration costs. Switching design tools mid-growth cycle incurs file conversion costs, workflow disruption, and team retraining that can exceed the annual subscription savings of a cheaper tool. The cheapest tool for a given year is not necessarily the cheapest tool over a three-year horizon when migration costs are included.

The third mistake is ignoring the per-user versus per-site distinction. Tools like Figma and Sketch charge per user, while Framer and Webflow charge per site. Teams with many designers but few pages benefit from per-user pricing, while teams with few designers but many pages benefit from per-site pricing. Matching the pricing model to the team's composition reduces unnecessary cost.
author: PilotStack Team
